title: Can Career Setbacks Actually Make You Stronger?
subtitle: 职业挫折的科学：失败如何让人更强大

paragraphs.en: How do early career setbacks affect our long-term success? Failures can help us learn and overcome our fears, but disasters can still wound us. Wouldn't it be nice if there was genuine, scientifically documented truth to the expression "what doesn't kill you makes you stronger"?
paragraphs.zh: 早期职业挫折如何影响我们的长期成功？失败可以帮助我们学习并克服恐惧，但灾难仍然会伤害我们。如果“杀不死你的东西会让你变得更坚强”这句话有真正的科学依据，那该多好？

paragraphs.en: One way social scientists have probed the effects of career setbacks is to look at scientists of very similar qualifications. These scientists, for reasons that are mostly arbitrary, either just missed getting a research grant or just barely made it. In social sciences, this is known as examining "near misses" and "narrow wins" in areas where merit is subjective.
paragraphs.zh: 社会科学家探究职业挫折影响的一种方法是观察资历非常相似的科学家。这些科学家出于大多是偶然的原因，要么刚好错过了研究资助，要么勉强获得了资助。在社会科学中，这被称为在成就评判较为主观的领域研究“惜败”和“险胜”。

paragraphs.en: That allows researchers to measure only the effects of being chosen or not. Studies in this area have found conflicting results. In the competitive game of biomedical science, research on scientists who narrowly lost or won grant money suggests that narrow winners become even bigger winners down the line — in other words, the rich get richer.
paragraphs.zh: 这使研究人员能够仅衡量是否被选中所产生的影响。该领域的研究发现了相互矛盾的结果。在生物医学科学的激烈竞争中，对以微弱差距胜出或落败的科学家的研究表明，险胜者后来会成为更大的赢家——换言之，富者愈富。

words.meaning: adj. 相互矛盾的，冲突的
adv. 勉强地，以微弱差距地

paragraphs.en: A 2018 study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, for example, followed researchers in the Netherlands. Researchers concluded that those who just barely qualified for a grant were able to get twice as much money within the next eight years as those who just missed out. The narrow winners were also 50 percent more likely to be given a professorship.
paragraphs.zh: 例如，2018年发表在《美国国家科学院院刊》上的一项研究追踪了荷兰的研究人员。研究人员得出结论，那些勉强获得资助的人在接下来的八年里获得的资金是刚好错过资助的人的两倍。险胜者获得教授职位的可能性也高出50%。

paragraphs.en: Others in the US have found similar effects with National Institutes of Health early-career fellowships, launching narrow winners far ahead of close losers. The phenomenon is often referred to as the Matthew effect, inspired by the Bible's wisdom that to those who have, more will be given.
paragraphs.zh: 美国的其他研究者在国立卫生研究院的早期职业奖学金中发现了类似的效应，险胜者远远领先于惜败者。这一现象通常被称为“马太效应”，灵感来自《圣经》中的智慧：凡有的，还要加给他更多。

words.meaning: n. 奖学金，研究员职位

paragraphs.en: There's a good explanation for the phenomenon in the book The Formula: The Universal Laws of Success by Albert-Laszlo Barabasi. According to Barabasi, it's easier and less risky for those in positions of power to hand awards and funding to those who've already been recognized. This is bad news for the losers — small early career setbacks seem to have a disproportionate effect down the line.
paragraphs.zh: 阿尔伯特-拉斯洛·巴拉巴西在其著作《巴拉巴西成功定律》中对这一现象给出了很好的解释。巴拉巴西认为，对于掌权者来说，将奖项和资金交给那些已获得认可的人更容易、风险也更小。这对失败者来说是坏消息——早期职业生涯中的小挫折似乎会在后续产生不成比例的影响。

words.meaning: adj. 不成比例的，不相称的

paragraphs.en: But other studies using the same technique have shown there's sometimes no penalty to a near miss. Students who just miss getting into top high schools or universities do just as well later in life as those who just manage to get accepted. In this case, what didn't kill them simply didn't matter.
paragraphs.zh: 但使用相同方法的其他研究表明，惜败有时并不会受到任何惩罚。那些刚好错过顶尖高中或大学的学生，在之后的人生中表现得和那些勉强被录取的学生一样好。在这种情况下，没有打败他们的东西根本无关紧要。

paragraphs.en: So is there any evidence that setbacks might actually improve our career prospects? There is now. In a study published in Nature Communications, Northwestern University sociologist Dashun Wang tracked more than 1,100 scientists who were on the border between getting a grant and missing out between 1990 and 2005.
paragraphs.zh: 那么，有没有证据表明挫折实际上可以改善我们的职业前景？现在有了。在发表于《自然通讯》的一项研究中，西北大学社会学家王大顺追踪了1990年至2005年间1100多名处于获得资助与错过资助边缘的科学家。

paragraphs.en: He followed various measures of performance over the next decade, including how many papers they authored and how influential those papers were, as measured by subsequent citations. As expected, there was a much higher rate of attrition among scientists who didn't get grants.
paragraphs.zh: 他追踪了接下来十年的各种绩效指标，包括他们发表了多少篇论文以及这些论文的影响力（以后续引用次数衡量）。正如预期的那样，未获得资助的科学家的流失率要高得多。

paragraphs.en: But among those who stayed on, the close losers performed even better than the narrow winners. To make sure this wasn't by chance, Wang conducted additional tests using different performance measures. He examined how many times people were first authors on influential studies, and the like.
paragraphs.zh: 但在那些坚持下来的人中，惜败者的表现甚至比险胜者更好。为了确保这不是偶然的，王使用不同的绩效指标进行了额外的测试，检查了人们作为有影响力研究的第一作者的次数等。

paragraphs.en: One straightforward reason close losers might outperform narrow winners is that the two groups have comparable ability. In Wang's study, he selected the most determined, passionate scientists from the loser group and culled what he deemed the weakest members of the winner group. Yet the persevering losers still came out on top.
paragraphs.zh: 惜败者可能超越险胜者的一个直接原因是两组人的能力相当。在王的研究中，他从失败者群体中挑选了最坚定、最有热情的科学家，并剔除了他认为获胜者群体中最弱的成员。然而，坚持不懈的失败者仍然名列前茅。

paragraphs.en: He thinks that being a close loser might give people a psychological boost, or the proverbial kick in the pants. Utrecht University sociologist Arnout van de Rijt, the lead author on the 2018 paper showing the rich get richer, said the new finding is apparently reasonable and worth some attention.
paragraphs.zh: 他认为，成为一名惜败者可能会给人带来心理上的激励，或者说是众所周知的“当头棒喝”。乌得勒支大学社会学家阿诺特·范德里特是2018年那篇关于“富者愈富”论文的主要作者，他表示这一新发现显然是合理的，值得关注。

words.meaning: adj. 众所周知的，谚语的

paragraphs.en: His own work showed that although the narrow winners did get much more money in the near future, the actual performance of the close losers was just as good. He said the people who should be paying regard to the Wang paper are the funding agents who distribute government grant money.
paragraphs.zh: 他自己的研究表明，虽然险胜者在不久的将来确实获得了更多资金，但惜败者的实际表现同样出色。他说，应该关注王的论文的人是那些分配政府补助金的资助机构。

paragraphs.en: After all, by continuing to pile riches on the narrow winners, the taxpayers are not getting the maximum bang for their buck if the close losers are performing just as well or even better. There's a huge amount of time and effort that goes into the process of selecting who gets grants, he said, and the latest research shows that the scientific establishment is not very good at distributing money.
paragraphs.zh: 毕竟，如果惜败者的表现同样好甚至更好，那么继续为险胜者锦上添花，纳税人就无法获得最大的回报。他说，选择谁获得资助的过程需要投入大量的时间和精力，而最新研究表明，科学界并不擅长分配资金。

paragraphs.en: "Maybe we should spend less money trying to figure out who is better than who," he said, suggesting that some more equal dividing up of money might be more productive and more efficient. Van de Rijt said he's not convinced that losing out gives people a psychological boost — it may yet be a selection effect.
paragraphs.zh: 他说：“也许我们应该少花钱去搞清楚谁比谁更好。”他建议更平等地分配资金可能会更有成效、更有效率。范德里特表示，他不确信失败会给人们带来心理上的激励——这可能仍然是一种选择效应。

words.meaning: adj. 富有成效的，多产的

paragraphs.en: Even though Wang tried to account for this by culling the weakest winners, it's impossible to know which of the winners would have quit had they found themselves on the losing side. For his part, Wang said that in his own experience, losing did light a motivating fire.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管王试图通过剔除最弱的获胜者来解释这一点，但我们不可能知道获胜者中有哪些人如果发现自己处于失败的一方会选择退出。就王本人而言，他说根据自己的经验，失败确实点燃了激励的火焰。

paragraphs.en: He recalled a recent paper he submitted to a journal, which accepted it only to request extensive editing, and then reversed course and rejected it. He submitted the unedited version to a more respected journal and got accepted.
paragraphs.zh: 他回忆起最近提交给一家期刊的一篇论文，该期刊接受后要求大量修改，随后又改变主意拒绝了。他将未经修改的版本提交给了一家更有声望的期刊，结果被接受了。

paragraphs.en: In sports and many areas of life, we think of failure as evidence of something we could have done better. We regard these disappointments as a fate we could have avoided with more careful preparation, different training, a better strategy, or more focus. And there it makes sense that failures show us the road to success.
paragraphs.zh: 在体育和生活的许多领域，我们将失败视为我们本可以做得更好的证据。我们认为这些失望是通过更仔细的准备、不同的训练、更好的策略或更多的专注就能避免的命运。从这个角度来说，失败为我们指明了成功之路。

paragraphs.en: These papers deal with a kind of failure people have little control over — rejection. Others determine who wins and who loses. But at the very least, the research is starting to show that early setbacks don't have to be fatal. They might even make us better at our jobs — getting paid like a winner, though, is a different matter.
paragraphs.zh: 这些论文探讨的是一种人们几乎无法控制的失败形式——被拒绝。谁赢谁输由他人决定。但至少，研究开始表明，早期的挫折不一定是致命的。它们甚至可能让我们在工作中表现更好——不过，能否获得与赢家同等的报酬，那就是另一回事了。
